nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and seaward reefs, along upper edges of coral-rich areas; found at depths of 0–30 m.

decorPreferences: deep sand bed (5–8 cm) for burrowing

diet.notes: Feeds on a wide variety of small benthic invertebrates as well as fish eggs. In captivity, has been observed accepting vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp and frozen brine shrimp; frequent small feedings are generally advised. Will tend to consume ornamental fan worms, small shrimp, and other small crustaceans.

goodWith: larger shrimp if introduced before wrasse
avoidWith: very small gobies or blennies that may be mistaken for prey

summary: A peaceful Indo-Pacific wrasse known for vivid adult coloration and dramatic juvenile color phases; generally considered reef-safe with corals but tends to consume ornamental invertebrates. Requires a well-established tank with a deep sand bed for burrowing.
careNotes:
The Dusky Wrasse (Halichoeres marginatus) inhabits lagoon and seaward reef habitats in the Indo-Pacific, from the Red Sea south to Mozambique and east to Hawaii, and has been documented in small groups or solitarily along coral-rich reef edges.

This species is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite — individuals are born female and dominant fish transition to male, establishing territories. Males display more vibrant coloration during spawning.

Juveniles are strikingly different from adults: juveniles are predominantly black with white longitudinal streaks and are encountered on exposed outer reef flats, while adults display blue, green, red, and yellow coloration. Juveniles are sometimes not recognized as the same species in the aquarium trade.

A deep sand bed of at least 5–8 cm is strongly advised, as this species has been documented burrowing into substrate to sleep and for protection. Without adequate substrate, stress and poor health outcomes have been reported.

Although often listed in reef-safe collections, the Dusky Wrasse is commonly considered safe with corals only — it tends to consume fan worms, small shrimp, small crabs, and other ornamental invertebrates. Caution is advised when keeping with small, desirable invertebrates.
breedingNotes: Has been documented as a pelagic spawner in the wild, with females migrating to established spawning sites where males hold territories. Protogynous sex change means lone fish or groups containing only females can produce a dominant male. Captive breeding has not been widely reported.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic spawner in the wild; captive breeding not widely achieved. Protogynous — dominant individual transitions from female to male.
